channel.title: maGaming RSS Feed - techraptor.net
channel.description: A cleaned-up version of the original gaming feed for techraptor.net

item.title: The Cast Of Vox Machina S4 Discuss Off Screen Moments, Plotline Selection, And Doty As Himself
item.content:encoded:
The Legend of Vox Machina is entering its penultimate season. After the defeat of the Chroma Conclave in Season 3, we're getting into the final arcs of Critical Role Campaign 1. Ahead of the release of Season 4, I had a chance to sit down with the cast of Critical Role and The Legend of Vox Machina to understand where we find our characters, how the serious tone maintains levity, and just what "Doty as Himself" even means…

The ending of Season 3, while a victory against the Chroma Conclave, did leave a bittersweet taste as Scanlan, played by Sam Riegel, left Vox Machina, so where we pick up with the party at the beginning of Season 4, now a year later, things aren't quite so happy.

Where are the Legends of Vox Machina at the top of Season 4?

Wanting to get a better idea of just where everyone's at, I asked the cast where we find each of their characters at the start of our tale. "Grog and Pike," Travis Willingham began, "are the ones that I think had to just stay behind and clean up behind the chateau party. Scanlan and Kaylie went off to do their own thing, Keyleth and Vax are off for her Aramente, the new De Rolo's - Vex and Percy - are off to Whitestone, and Grog and Pike are like 'Cool, we're just gonna stay here, we'll cover the tab.'"

Marisha Ray, who plays the Half-Elf Druid Keyleth of the Air Ashari, concurred that Willingham summed it all up perfectly.

Willingham slipped into the voice of Grog Strongjaw, the Goliath Barbarian, playing up a sense of searching for purpose. Ashley Johnson added to the skit as Pike Trickfoot, the Gnome Cleric, reassuring the rest of a departing Vox Machina, "Yeah, we'll be real busy."

Johnson continued, "I think we realize early on in the season that Pike is struggling a bit, and is not in the best place, and has lost her purpose and is missing her found family." While no stranger to questioning her fate and the influence that her somewhat-absent god has on her life, this season sees a lot of trials and tribulations for Pike Trickfoot.

Taliesin Jaffe, playing human Gunslinger Percival De Rolo, summarized that "Percy is living a calm domestic life in Whitestone, relearning how to be a noble again and tinkering with the tiny sphere of death in his basement."

Passing the torch onto Laura Bailey, who plays Vex'ahlia the half-elf Ranger/Rogue, Vex is "also living a fabulous domestic life settling in to nobility quite well, but also her heart is aching a little bit because she's missing the freedom of the life she had with her brother, and she misses her brother most of all."

Her bother, Vax'ildan is a half-elf Rogue/Paladin, played by Liam O'Brien followed up "He is off juggling both the question he has about the condition he's carried over from the end of last season and what mystery is tied to his fate and the Matron, but also he's with the woman he loves and they're playing 'Around The World In 80 Days' as she goes on her self-actualization journey so he's trying to live and laugh and love and juggle those two sides of the coin.

For Scanlan, the Gnome Bard played by Sam Riegel, it's added that "Scanlan is out there in the world, somewhere, doing something… you'll find out when you tune in to Season 4."

As the DM of the campaign, Matt Mercer might not have a specific character to play, but you'll hear him throughout each episode, and sometimes even see him. With gravitas, he told me, "I begin this season as the wind and the water, the life of the sky and the moon of night. I am Exandria, and I remain unchanged."

Willingham let out a prolonged "Daaaamn."

Restructuring Actual Play To TV Show

We find Vox Machina in different pairings: Grog and Pike, Vax and Keyleth, and Vex and Percy. I was interested to hear what decisions went into splitting the party in such a way that just wouldn't be possible for a group of people sitting around a table playing Dungeons & Dragons.

"Well, we definitely think it makes for an interesting story, but I would be lying if I didn't say Animation Production doesn't factor into it heavily." Willingham let out a laugh, "We were six, seven party members and a freaking bear, and very early we heard back, 'Hey, quadrapeds? They suck when there's six other pairs of legs to draw.' So we had to get really creative with when and where Trinket would be, and also if there were opportunities to split up the party to make a more interesting story."

Willingham explained that as writers, they loved the feedback to look for those times where it would make more sense to send just a small number of party members, "and just ran with that torch. You know, when you don't have the entire party with you, the stakes can be a little bit higher, and I think the drama of that presented itself in a very natural way."

As the party was split, I did wonder where it was in the writing process that the positions of each character for their major moments were decided, and what level of control the cast had on getting to say what they would be showing up for.

"We all participate in the crafting of the season and the writing of the show," Riegel explained, "obviously, changes are always made, characters need to be moved around, and timelines shifted. We always start the beginning of any writer's room by asking the members of Critical Role 'What's most important to you, what do you need to see in this season, what moments, what lines, what story beats/arcs do we need to hit or else you will be mad forever if you don't?"

Riegel also said there was time to also pose ideas of things that didn't happen in the original season, that they might like to see. An easy example he gave was "I want to see two bards battle each other with music" or "I want to see Grog get smart."

"I want to see Percy dead for a couple of episodes," Jaffe jokingly threw out

Behind The DM Screen Of Campaign 1

Just like how a traditional D&D game wouldn't be able to have whole adventures with just two members, Season 4 also lets us know what's going on with the Cult of the Whispered One while Vox Machina is following up on leads. I was interested to hear from Mercer whether what we're seeing on screen was things that had always transpired behind the scenes of Campaign 1, or whether these moments were invented just for the show.

"Yes and…" Mercer began, "a lot of this is me going back to old notes or me going to things that were already in my mind and being able to say it outside of conversations post-campaign when we're all getting drinks and the players are shooting me with questions of like '…and what was actually happening there?' 'What was happening during this time?' and now I get to do that with the rest of the writers' room."

"For me it's really cool, much in the way we do for The Mighty Nein as well, to show different perspectives that othewise just lived in my mind or I could allude to through in-game moments, but also bring in wonderful outside perspectives of people that haven't been so close to this work who can bring in a bunch of unique ideas and can take what already exists and ramp it further. It's been a wonderful collaborative process of taking these seeds of imagination and watching them germinate uniquely for this particular format of telling the story."

Speaking further to Mercer's point, but from the other side of the DM screen, Ray added "I think Matt has always been really good, even when we're sitting around the table in the original campaigns, in letting us know 'Hey, you can take time to go on a bar crawl or go off on this other wild goose chase but the world will keep spinning and other machinations will keep developing whether you address them or not.' I think that [The Legend of Vox Machina] just gives you that opportunity to pull out from that first-person perspective and show what he was always alluding to."

Ray began to nervously giggle as she reflected on the constant narrative threat that hung over the party, especially as the Cult of the Whispered One grew more powerful.

There's a bit more to this interview discussing a moment from mid-season, if you're interested in learning about that come back on June 17th

A season focusing on the rise of a death cult brings with it a lot of serious topics: revenge, mortal destiny, and of course… death. That's not to say that after some trauma and bloodshed, there isn't still a chance for levity. Asking how they balance the tone to maintain the high stakes of the season but also the vibe of "an adventuring party of friends," Jaffe gave the simple answer that he "doesn't know if they're capable of not being silly, just as people, and since we're crafting the story, I don't know if we would be able to get through it."

We're not very serious people, lets be honest - Taliesin Jaffe

"I think the only way to be able to get through it," Bailey paused, "is like laughing at a funeral."

O'Brien added, "We also purposefully look for ways to add levity to the season in addition to being a bunch of nitwits."

Guests Returning From The Actual Play, New Voices Arriving

This season has not only welcomed some incredibly big names to The Legend of Vox Machina, like Wayne Brady as Taryon Darrington and Andy Serkis, but guests who joined the table temporarily, like Jon Heder, were able to return and reprise their roles.

"It was a delight to have Jon [Heder] immediately say yes," Willingham said. From his recounting of the story, it seemed like there was excitement and surprise as he asked, "Am I going to be playing the same guy?" to a resounding yes.

Speaking on the new voices, Willingham added: "To reach out to Wayne Brady, who we've known for years, and to hear that he's a fan of what we do as well and that he was excited to jump in and tackle the show is not just an honor but the highest of compliments."

"We have the amazing Andy Serkis coming in, who we're obviously enormous fans of, and we just continue to collect as many Lord of the Rings pieces as we possibly can, and really it was just trying to decide what kind of voice or performance we wanted from Andy because he's kind of capable of doing anything."

Other Lord of the Rings alumni who have joined the cast of The Legends of Vox Machina include Dominic Monaghan and Billy Boyd.

We think folks will be delighted and surprised by [Andy Serkis's] performance - Travis Willingham

"I also feel compelled to shout out Tom Cardy as the dark bard," Ray added, referencing the Australian Comedian and Musician. There were cheers throughout the Zoom call at the mention of Cardy's name.

"Speaking to what you were saying, Andy, of bringing in people who've become a part of the extended Critical Role family, we spent two weeks with Tom in Australia, so to be able to bring him in here is great."

Adding further shoutouts to other members of the Critical Role family, Willingham highlighted the work of Anjali Bhimani and Chris Prynoski – President of Titmouse, the animation studio creating The Legend of Vox Machina – "so the keen-eared will have to keep their ears open for those voices.

Riegel, who also wears the hat of Casting Director, breathed a sigh of relief, saying, "It's so much easier now because the show exists. For the first Season of the show, it was hard to get people to say they'd join our cast because the show didn't exist, and it was a long explanation."

It's based on a live play roleplaying game that's on Twitch and YouTube… but no, it's good trust us!

"Now we can point to the show and say, 'Hey, do you want to be in this?' and they are like, 'yeah, that's sick.' We've been very fortunate that a lot of the actors we've got to work with have heard of Critical Role or The Legend of Vox Machina or The Mighty Nein, and if they haven't, their assistant, or their kids, or their neighbors have."

"I would love to brag that I was the greatest casting director in history, but it wasn't that hard. We just showed them the show, sent them the script, and they were like 'yeah, this sounds amazing'"

As a lover of voice acting and scouring credits for all of the familiar names from different projects, I will always scan through the credits of shows. What caught my eye is that, as part of the main cast, the actor for Doty, Taryon Darrington's robot companion, is always listed as himself.

Pondering just what that meant, I knew if there was anyone who could get me an answer, it would be Sam Riegel, I wasn't expecting the response I got…

"Doty, the voice of Doty… is Doty."

I joked, "Does that mean Doty will be getting his own IMDB profile page?"

"Correct," Sam continued, "I will say that this is me, I did that. I had to go through Amazon's legal department, and we had to call SAG. Initially, they were like 'No, you can't say Doty played himself' and I was like 'But I want to' and so they did, and all kinds of contracts and letters had to be signed, and so Doty is played by himself."

O'Brien joked that "the machines are becoming sentient," while Jaffe chuckled, exclaiming that he didn't know this story about Doty.

The Legend of Vox Machina Season 4 will air on Prime Video on June 3rd with weekly releases. The full season will be 12 episodes long, and don't forget to return to learn more about episode 7. After Season 4, there's one more season left for Vox Machina to wrap up the campaign, but thankfully, it's already been greenlit and is well into production.



https://techraptor.net/tabletop/features/cast-of-vox-machina-s4-discuss-behind-dm-screen-moments-picking-plotlines-and
item.dc:creator: invalid@example.com (ResetEra_Newsbot
